(Alternative theory) {{ill|King Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} |Artifacts = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa |ImperialTomb = {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation: "Ega Nagano Nishi no Misasagi" (恵我長野西陵) |Notes = Ranked 16th largest kofun in Japan[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Kofun Size Ranking (Nationwide)] (Sakai City Homepage, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Osaka city |Label = Oka
Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Okamisanzai Full view of the mound (right: front section, left rear: circular rear section) |BelongsTo = {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, Fujidera 4-chome |LatitudeDegrees = 34|LatitudeMinutes = 33|LatitudeSeconds = 55.35 |LongitudeDegrees = 135|LongitudeMinutes = 35|LongitudeSeconds = 38.60 |ISO = JP-27 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Dimensions = Mound length: 245m
Height: 20m (circular rear section) |ConstructionPeriod = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |BurialFacility = Unknown (possibly {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}) |AccompanyingMounds = 1 |BurialPerson = ({{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation) {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}
(Alternative theory) {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}
(Alternative theory) {{ill|King Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} |Artifacts = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa |ImperialTomb = {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation: "Ega Nagano Nishi no Misasagi" (恵我長野西陵) |Notes = Ranked 16th largest kofun in Japan[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Kofun Size Ranking (Nationwide)] (Sakai City Homepage, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Osaka city |Label = Oka
Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped burial mound ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the burial mounds comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest burial mound.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} (Alternative theory) {{ill|King Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} |Artifacts = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa |ImperialTomb = {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation: "Ega Nagano Nishi no Misasagi" (恵我長野西陵) |Notes = Ranked 16th largest kofun in Japan[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Kofun Size Ranking (Nationwide)] (Sakai City Homepage, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Osaka city |Label = Oka
Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]]{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Oka Misanzai Kofun |image = [[File:Oka Misanzai Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (right: front section, left rear: circular rear section) |BelongsTo = {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, Fujidera 4-chome |LatitudeDegrees = 34|LatitudeMinutes = 33|LatitudeSeconds = 55.35 |LongitudeDegrees = 135|LongitudeMinutes = 35|LongitudeSeconds = 38.60 |ISO = JP-27 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Dimensions = Mound length: 245m
Height: 20m (circular rear section) |ConstructionPeriod = Late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |BurialFacility = Unknown (possibly {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}) |AccompanyingMounds = 1 |BurialPerson = ({{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation) {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}
(Alternative theory) {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}
(Alternative theory) {{ill|King Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} |Artifacts = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa |ImperialTomb = {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation: "Ega Nagano Nishi no Misasagi" (恵我長野西陵) |Notes = Ranked 16th largest kofun in Japan[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Kofun Size Ranking (Nationwide)] (Sakai City Homepage, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Osaka city |Label = Oka
Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} (Alternative theory) {{ill|King Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} |Artifacts = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa |ImperialTomb = {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designation: "Ega Nagano Nishi no Misasagi" (恵我長野西陵) |Notes = Ranked 16th largest kofun in Japan[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Kofun Size Ranking (Nationwide)] (Sakai City Homepage, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Osaka city |Label = Oka
Misanzai
Kofun |Icon = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-SSW |LabelPosition = Top }} '''Oka Misanzai Kofun''' (岡ミサンザイ古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in Fujidera, Osaka Prefecture. It is a keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) and one of the Kofuns comprising the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}}, a World Cultural Heritage site. Although the identity of the individual buried within the kofun is unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated it as the tomb of the 14th emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}''', naming it the "Ega no Nagano no Nishi no Misasagi" (惠我長野西陵). Nationally, this kofun is the 16th largest Kofun.[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Tomb Size Ranking (National)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). Archaeological evidence suggests that it was constructed in the late 5th century, during the middle {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Many researchers now propose that the burial site is the true tomb of the 21st emperor, '''{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}''', or of {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}}. == Overview == Located at the western edge of the Furuichi Kofun Group in eastern Osaka Prefecture, Oka Misanzai Kofun is a massive keyhole-shaped mound. The name "Oka" derives from the local area, while "Misanzai" is a corruption of the term "Misasagi" (imperial tomb). The mound has undergone significant modifications due to the construction of a medieval fortress and repairs made during the late Edo period.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳|2014|pp=111-124}} Currently, the site is managed by the Imperial Household Agency as Emperor Chūai’s mausoleum. The mound is oriented with the square front facing south-southwest.{{Harvid|仲哀天皇陵古墳（古墳）|1989}} Originally constructed in three tiers, the mound was altered to a five-tier structure during restoration. The outer layer of the mound contains fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, but no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} has been found. A platform on the eastern side of the constriction (with the presence of a corresponding western platform uncertain) has also been identified. A wide moat (50 meters across along the main axis) encircles the mound, with an earthen embankment (20 meters wide) on its outer perimeter, adorned with rows of cylindrical haniwa. Based on the shape of the mound, the burial chamber is speculated to be a {{ill|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Haniwa fragments suggest that the mound was constructed in the late 5th century, a period marked by a shift from earlier kofun traditions, as reflected in the cessation of fukiishi and the adoption of the yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Historical Background == In {{ill|Bunkyū|ja|文久}} 3 (1863), the mound was designated as Emperor Chūai’s tomb by {{ill|Toda Yamato no Kami|ja|戸田大和守}}. In {{ill|Genji|ja|元治}} 1 (1864)-{{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 1 (1865), restoration work was undertaken. However, modern research questions the attribution to Emperor Chūai, with several scholars favoring Emperor Yūryaku or Wō (King of Wa) based on historical and archaeological evidence. The site has also been associated with medieval fortifications and played a potential role during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|lt=Summer Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂夏の陣}}, where it might have served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''' * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from Fujidera Station on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Bu of Wa|ja|武 (倭王)}} served as a strategic base.{{Harvid|千田2017年}} == Burial Facilities == No definitive burial chamber has been excavated, but the mound is hypothesized to contain a yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu. == Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 245 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 150 meters ** Height: 20 meters * Front square section: ** Width: 180 meters ** Height: 16.6 meters This makes Oka Misanzai Kofun the third-largest mound in the Furuichi Kofun Group and the 16th-largest nationwide. == Surrounding Tombs == Nearby, the {{ill|Hachizuka Kofun|ja|鉢塚古墳}}, located to the north of the mound's rear section, is considered a {{ill|Baizuka|ja|陪塚}} (subsidiary mound). == Modern Access == '''location''' * Fujidera 4-chome, Fujidera City, Osaka
